html:
AQA GCSE English Literature — Love and Relationships Anthology — 10 Most Likely Comparison Questions to Come Up (2026)

AQA English Literature Paper 2 — Section B (Anthology Poetry) Q25. 30 marks. Recommended writing time: 45 minutes. AOs: AO1 (response + textual reference) / AO2 (writer's methods) / AO3 (context). AQA prints ONE anchor poem in full on the paper and names it in the stem; the STUDENT picks any other poem from the anthology to compare. Stem opens "Compare how poets present [THEME] in '[NAMED ANCHOR POEM]' and in one other poem from 'Love and relationships'.". The real 2018 paper opened: "Compare how poets present powerful feelings about love in 'Neutral Tones' and in one other poem from 'Love and relationships'."

How this list was generated

This Q-bank was built using Sophicly's AQA-Prediction-Algorithm adapted for the Love and Relationships cluster's comparative-question shape (see `Love-and-Relationships-AQA-Prediction-Algorithm.md` in this folder). The algorithm scores each of the 15 anthology poems on six signals — frequency as anchor in past papers, cycle gap since last anchored, coverage gap (poems never anchored in the 2017-2025 window), unexplored-angle signal (unexplored stem-themes for that poem), core-topic boost (the five core poems AQA returns to most), and an exact-stem-repeat penalty. The cluster has set nine of fifteen poems as anchor across 2017-2025 with no repeats; six poems remain never-set within window and are therefore strong candidates for 2026 even when they are not on the spine. Spine poems (Porphyria's Lover, The Farmer's Bride, Mother Any Distance, Before You Were Mine, Walking Away) get core-topic boost despite earlier-window appearances, because AQA cannot avoid the cluster's pedagogical core forever.

Section IDs (Q1–Q10) ARE the priority order. This differs from the per-text Q-banks, where the section IDs are stable references and a separate priority table reorders them. Because the L&R anchor-poem ranking is the document's spine, Q1 = highest-confidence anchor, Q10 = lowest within the top 10.

Priority order — rehearse in this order

The ranking below predicts the LIKELY NAMED ANCHOR POEM for the 2026 paper. AQA prints one anchor in full and names it in the stem; the student picks the comparison. The "Sophicly's suggested comparison" column is our recommended pairing (not what AQA pairs — AQA does not pair). The "Alternative comparison candidates" lists 2-3 other defensible pairings the student could substitute.

| Rank | Predicted anchor poem | Confidence | Suggested 2026 stem theme | Sophicly's suggested comparison | Alternative comparison candidates |

|---:|---|---|---|---|---|

| 1 | Porphyria's Lover (Browning, 1836) | HIGH | Possessive love / power and control in relationships | The Farmer's Bride (Mew) | Neutral Tones (Hardy) · Before You Were Mine (Duffy) — possessive edge |

| 2 | The Farmer's Bride (Mew, 1916) | HIGH | Power and control in relationships / female silence | Porphyria's Lover (Browning) | Neutral Tones (Hardy) · Winter Swans (Sheers) — contrast pair |

| 3 | Before You Were Mine (Duffy, 1993) | HIGH | Memory in relationships / parental love | Eden Rock (Causley) | Follower (Heaney) · Climbing My Grandfather (Waterhouse) |

| 4 | Walking Away (Day Lewis, 1962) | MED-HIGH | Parental love / letting go | Mother, Any Distance (Armitage) | Eden Rock (Causley) · Follower (Heaney) |

| 5 | Mother, Any Distance (Armitage, 1993) | MED-HIGH | Parental love / growing up | Walking Away (Day Lewis) | Letters from Yorkshire (Dooley) · Follower (Heaney) |

| 6 | Love's Philosophy (Shelley, 1819) | MED | Strong feelings / desire and longing | Sonnet 29 (Barrett Browning) | Porphyria's Lover (Browning) — contrast · Winter Swans (Sheers) |

| 7 | Letters from Yorkshire (Dooley, 1997) | MED | Distance in relationships | Mother, Any Distance (Armitage) | Winter Swans (Sheers) · When We Two Parted (Byron) |

| 8 | Climbing My Grandfather (Waterhouse, 2002) | MED | Family relationships / understanding | Follower (Heaney) | Eden Rock (Causley) · Mother, Any Distance (Armitage) |

| 9 | Sonnet 29 (Barrett Browning, c.1845-46) | MED | Strong feelings / desire | Love's Philosophy (Shelley) | Porphyria's Lover (Browning) — contrast · Winter Swans (Sheers) |

| 10 | Neutral Tones (Hardy, 1867) | MED | Lost love / memory | When We Two Parted (Byron) | The Farmer's Bride (Mew) · Letters from Yorkshire (Dooley) |

Coverage Pack (rehearse but not top-10):

• When We Two Parted (Byron, 1816) — set 2021 at loss/grief; recency rebuilding.

• Singh Song! (Nagra, 2007) — set 2022 at joyful love; recency-penalised.

• Eden Rock (Causley, 1988) — set 2023 at memory/family; recency-penalised.

• Follower (Heaney, 1966) — set 2024 at family relationships; recency-penalised.

• Winter Swans (Sheers, 2005) — set 2025 at reconciliation; recency-penalised.

How to use this document

How the AQA Q25 stem actually works. AQA names ONE anchor poem in the stem and prints that poem in full on the exam paper. The student then picks ANY OTHER poem from the Love and Relationships anthology (all fifteen are eligible) to compare against the anchor. The stem shape is fixed: "Compare how poets present [THEME] in '[NAMED ANCHOR POEM]' and in one other poem from 'Love and relationships'." Real 2018 example: "Compare how poets present powerful feelings about love in 'Neutral Tones' and in one other poem from 'Love and relationships'."

What this Q-bank predicts. Each Q1–Q10 below predicts the LIKELY anchor poem the 2026 paper will name. The "Suggested comparison poem" line under each stem is Sophicly's recommended pairing — NOT what AQA pairs (AQA does not pair). The "Alternative comparison candidates" line lists 2-3 other defensible pairings the student could substitute. Students are free to substitute any other anthology poem they have rehearsed; the plan structure (BP1 = Form comparison / BP2 = Structure comparison / BP3 = Language comparison, every BP weaving both poems through Comparative TTECEA+C) remains valid whichever comparison the student selects.

Each question prints the AQA-style stem, our suggested comparison plus alternatives, a one-paragraph frame, a short technique gloss, and a skeleton plan in Sophicly's locked comparison structure (Introduction 3 sentences / three Body Paragraphs of 7 sentences / Conclusion 4 sentences) adapted for poetry comparison. If exam time is tight, rehearse the top 5 first (Q1–Q5). For comprehensive coverage, rehearse all 10. Memorise the cross-question reuse table at the end so the same prepared pairings serve multiple Qs.

Pedagogical depth — three non-negotiables for HIGH-confidence picks

Every Grade-9 essay on an L&R comparison should thread at least one of these moves into one BP or the conclusion. They are the cluster's deepest analytical pivots; Sophia teaches them once so students can deploy them across any Q.

1. The anthology's theme is NOT love — it is POWER inside love. The 1882 MWPA is the historical hinge.

The standard reading of Love and Relationships identifies the cluster as a comparison-set on forms of love — romantic / parental / filial / lost. The sharper counter-reading exposes the cluster's deepest editorial argument: love is not the theme — POWER INSIDE LOVE is. Porphyria's Lover, The Farmer's Bride, and My Last Duchess (in the parallel P&C anthology) all stage love-as-possession against coverture's property-marriage logic; Sonnet 29, Singh Song!, and Winter Swans all stage love-as-mutual-equality. The 1882 Married Women's Property Act (general reform; the 1870 Act was limited) is the historical hinge that divides the cluster: pre-MWPA poems show love as coverture (the wife as legal property merged into the husband's identity); post-MWPA / contemporary poems show love as negotiated equality between separate legal persons. Porphyria's Lover (1836) and The Farmer's Bride (1916, rural reality lagging the legislation) belong to the pre-MWPA half; Sonnet 29 (composed 1845-46, published 1850) sits on the hinge as Barrett Browning's counter-volta against the coverture default; Singh Song! (2007) and Winter Swans (2005) belong to the post-MWPA half. Critical framing must avoid feminist-critique vocabulary. Frame instead as property-marriage law (the specific legal regime), coverture (the legal doctrine's technical name), dynastic marriage / honour culture (the social structures that produced the legal regime), or the wife as legal property (the descriptive consequence). This framing is more historically precise than the loaded vocabulary and keeps the critique of the social arrangement intact while staying within historicist register. Thread this into: Q1 Porphyria's Lover / Q2 The Farmer's Bride / Q9 Sonnet 29 / any BP3 that pairs a pre-MWPA poem with a post-MWPA poem; any conclusion that names the cluster's editorial argument.

2. Parental-love poems are not about love — they are about the speaker processing the parent's mortality.

The standard reading of the cluster's parental-love poems (Walking Away, Eden Rock, Mother, Any Distance, Before You Were Mine, Climbing My Grandfather, Follower) identifies them as celebrations of the parent-child bond. The sharper counter-reading exposes the cluster's deepest temporal argument: these poems are not about love between parent and child — they are about the SPEAKER processing the parent's mortality. Walking Away anticipates loss — Day Lewis writes from the parent-side, the closing tercet "selfhood begins with a walking away, / And love is proved in the letting go" names love as the anticipation of permanent separation. Eden Rock the dead parents wait beyond the stream — Causley's father died when Causley was a child; the closing line "I had not thought that it would be like this" names the speaker's recognition that what he had not understood as a child he now must inherit. Mother, Any Distance the tape-measure runs out, the umbilical metaphor breaks — Armitage's curtal-sonnet's fifteenth line falls short of the conventional fourteen. Before You Were Mine reaches back before the daughter existed — Duffy constructs the mother as the 1950s teenager she cannot otherwise know. Climbing My Grandfather the speaker maps the grandfather's body as terrain that will not endure. Follower the closing inversion — "It is my father who keeps stumbling / Behind me, and will not go away" — names the role-reversal age has produced. Parental love in the anthology is always already mourning: the speaker registers the parent at the point at which the parent will, has, or shortly must leave. Read this way, the cluster's parental poems are temporal lyrics about the asymmetry between the parent's generation and the speaker's own. Thread this into: Q3 Before You Were Mine / Q4 Walking Away / Q5 Mother, Any Distance / Q7 Letters from Yorkshire / Q8 Climbing My Grandfather / any conclusion that names the cluster's temporal argument.

3. Name WHICH love — the precondition of the comparison's analytical work.

The anthology's overarching theme is love and relationships, but AQA's "love" stem is multivalent. The cluster anatomises romantic-erotic love (Shelley, Byron, Barrett Browning, Sheers), possessive love (R. Browning, Mew, Duffy at its edge), parental love (Day Lewis, Armitage, Duffy, Waterhouse, Causley), filial love (Heaney, Armitage, Waterhouse, Duffy, Causley), familial-by-blood love (Heaney, Causley, Waterhouse), familial-by-marriage / cross-cultural love (Nagra), mourned love (Byron, Hardy), and long-distance love (Dooley, Day Lewis, Causley, Armitage). Every comparison essay should name in its thesis WHICH love the two poems are about and HOW that love operates differently in each — generic "love" answers receive Band 4 ceiling. Top-band answers specify and discriminate. The specification is the precondition of the comparison's analytical work; without it, the essay catalogues techniques in two poems without naming what they are techniques OF.

Why this depth matters pedagogically. AQA's Band 6 descriptor asks for "a convincing, critical analysis and exploration" of method, sustained across the comparison. A reading that exposes POWER inside love (not love itself) as the cluster's theme, names parental-love poems as temporal lyrics about mortality (not celebrations of the bond), and grounds the social-arrangement critique in MWPA-historical specificity is the species of conceptualisation Examiner Reports name as the top-band signature. Without it, a comparison essay is parallel description; with it, the essay is critical analysis.

A universal critical-angle method. Every text in the AQA spec rewards the same move: identify the standard reading; then identify a counter-reading the same evidence supports; then read the two together as the species of conceptualisation Grade 9 demands. For Love and Relationships the dual readings are love-as-emotion/POWER-inside-love, parental-bond/mortality-processing, single-theme/specified-form. Sophia should generate the second reading whenever helping a student plan — the standard reading is what most students will write; the second reading is what carries them past the band-9 line.

The seven-sentence body paragraph shape — integrated-comparison Comparative TTECEA+C

AQA Q25 rewards INTEGRATED COMPARISON, not block comparison. Every BP weaves both poems together — BP1 = Form comparison / BP2 = Structure comparison / BP3 = Language comparison. Every sentence references both poems; no sentence is pure single-poem description. Each BP follows Comparative TTECEA+C — seven moves, each comparative.

1. Comparative Topic sentence — pure concept, no techniques named (not even form labels). Opens with "While [Poet A]..., [Poet B]..." or "Where [Poet A]..., [Poet B]..." — explicit conceptual contrast about how each poem handles this dimension (Form / Structure / Language). ~30 words max.

2. Comparative Technique — name the form/structure/language technique in each poem inside a single move that quotes both and infers what the pair achieves; sophisticated literary terms (dramatic monologue, curtal sonnet, Petrarchan sonnet, cinquain, envelope rhyme, pathetic fallacy, extended conceit, polyptoton, refrain, volta, code-switching, half-rhyme, enjambment, caesura).

3. Comparative Evidence — one anchor quote per poem, paired and inferred together; the quotes are the same evidence the technique sentence cites or close-paired equivalents.

4. Comparative Close Analysis — zoom into specific word / sound / line-break in each poem, woven; "where [Poet A]'s [word] does X, [Poet B]'s [word] does Y".

5. Comparative Effects on reader — what each poem makes the reader register, compared in a single move; specific emotion words (revulsion, complicity, tenderness, dread, awe, recognition, claustrophobia, suspension, longing, grief, joy). Avoid generic "sadness" / "interest".

6. Comparative Author's purpose — hedged ("may have written", "can be read as"); names why each poet chose this form / structure / language differently in one breath.

7. Comparative Context (AO3) — one period-specific fact per poet, paired; context drives the choice each poet made on this dimension.

Memorise these once

The Love and Relationships anthology argues that love is not one thing but a plural set of relationships — romantic, possessive, familial, generational, geographic, equal, lost, surviving — and that what changes across the cluster is not the emotion itself but the conditions under which the emotion is allowed to be voiced. Across fifteen poems written between 1815 and 2007, the cluster moves from Romantic-period secrecy and decorum (Byron, Shelley) through Victorian possession under coverture marriage (Browning, Mew, Barrett Browning's counter-volta) into modern parental letting-go on both sides (Day Lewis, Armitage), constructive memory of absent or pre-maternal parents (Causley, Duffy, Waterhouse), filial admiration with role reversal (Heaney), long-distance correspondence (Dooley), reconciliation after quarrel (Sheers), and joyful intercultural celebration (Nagra). Two governing tensions run through the cluster: VOICE (who gets to speak about love and who is silenced) and CONDITIONS (whether the social arrangement permits the love to be honestly named).

Key terms (memorise once — useful across multiple Qs):

• Dramatic monologue — verse spoken by a constructed persona whose worldview the form exposes (Porphyria's Lover; The Farmer's Bride; partially Love's Philosophy in its second reading).

• Curtal sonnet — a curtailed or shortened sonnet (Mother, Any Distance — fifteen lines rather than fourteen, ending on a short line that enacts curtailment).

• Petrarchan sonnet — fourteen-line form with octave-sestet structure pivoting on a volta (Sonnet 29 — with the volta arriving deliberately early).

• Cinquain — five-line stanza (Porphyria's Lover at the structural level; Before You Were Mine at the visible-stanza level).

• Praeteritio — saying something by saying you will not say it (signature move of Porphyria's Lover and My Last Duchess in the parallel anthology).

• Pathetic fallacy — natural phenomena attributed human emotion (Porphyria's "sullen wind"; Neutral Tones' "chidden of God" sun; Winter Swans' "waterlogged earth gulping for breath").

• Extended conceit — a metaphor sustained throughout the poem (Mother, Any Distance's tape-measure as umbilical cord; Climbing My Grandfather's mountaineering of the grandfather's body; Sonnet 29's vines and tree).

• Polyptoton — repetition of a word in different grammatical forms (Byron's "They know not I knew thee, / Who knew thee too well").

• Refrain — a phrase repeated structurally (Duffy's "before you were mine" three times; Nagra's "vee share in chapatti / vee share in di chutney").

• Code-switching — alternation between languages or dialects within a single utterance (Nagra's Punjabi-English Punglish; Agard in the parallel anthology).

• Cyclical structure — final image returning to opening (Byron's "silence and tears"; Hardy's pond; Browning's interrupted weather).

Cluster-specific framing (memorise once — threads into every Love and Relationships BP):

• Romantic-period secrecy — Byron's When We Two Parted and Shelley's Love's Philosophy both write under conditions of social decorum that constrained how feeling could be voiced. Aristocratic codes demanded composure; pantheistic philosophy weaponised cosmic argument as social pressure.

• Coverture marriage / property-marriage logic — Browning's Porphyria's Lover (1836) and Mew's The Farmer's Bride (1916) both stage relationships in which the legal and social arrangement treated the woman as the husband's property; Browning's speaker takes this logic to its lethal endpoint, Mew's farmer to the threshold of further violence. The Married Women's Property Acts of 1870 and 1882 ended coverture in legal theory only.

• Mid twentieth-century parental letting-go — Day Lewis's Walking Away (1962, parent side) and Armitage's Mother, Any Distance (1993, son side) bookend the same threshold moment. Both belong to a mid-to-late twentieth-century English meditative tradition (Wordsworth, Edward Thomas, Larkin, Armitage) in which personal experience is processed into universal wisdom.

• The audacious reconstruction of an absent parent — Causley's Eden Rock (1988) and Duffy's Before You Were Mine (1993) both construct a parent the speaker cannot remember adult-knowing. Causley's father died when Causley was a child; Duffy's mother existed before the daughter existed. Both poems' audacious imaginative reach is the form's central rhetorical move.

• Intercultural celebration as cultural work — Nagra's Singh Song! (2007) is the cluster's only openly joyful, publicly performable love poem. Its Punjabi-English Punglish refuses translation and reclaims hybrid voice as legitimate poetic register.

---

Q1 — Porphyria's Lover (predicted anchor) [HIGH confidence — never set as anchor in 9-year window]

Frame

Browning's 1836 dramatic monologue and Mew's 1916 dramatic monologue both stage possessive love through a male speaker whose own language exposes him, but their endpoints differ: Browning's speaker completes the violence his monologue prepares while Mew's farmer remains suspended on the threshold of further violence the poem refuses to narrate. The plan traces the completed-versus-threshold differential — the shared dramatic-monologue form producing opposite tonal positions on the same possessive logic.

Pedagogical depth to thread. Coverture marriage and property-marriage logic. Browning writes in 1836, decades before the Married Women's Property Acts of 1870 and 1882; Mew writes in 1916, when the legal doctrine has been reformed but rural reality lags the law. Both poems anatomise the social arrangement that treats the wife as the husband's property, the difference being that Browning's poem takes the logic to its lethal endpoint and Mew's exposes its slower domestic damage. Land this in BP3 or the conclusion.

Technique gloss:

• Dramatic monologue (Browning, Mew) — verse spoken by a constructed persona whose worldview the form exposes.

• Cinquain with ABABB rhyme (Browning) — five-line stanzas with asymmetrical rhyme encoding imbalance into the form.

• Pathetic fallacy (Browning, Mew) — Browning's "sullen wind" projecting the speaker's mind; Mew's "short days shorten" mirroring seasonal emotional decline.

• Triple repetition (Browning) — "That moment she was mine, mine, fair".

• Reported speech rupturing the monologue (Mew) — the bride's single utterance "Not near, not near!" — the only direct voice she is given.

• Closing fragmentation (Mew) — "Her eyes, her hair, her hair!" leaving the reader at the threshold of further violence.

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): When Robert Browning published Porphyria's Lover anonymously in 1836, the legal doctrine of coverture — codified in Blackstone's Commentaries (1765) — was still in full effect across England, meaning that a married woman's legal identity merged into her husband's at the altar; Charlotte Mew published The Farmer's Bride eighty years later, after the Married Women's Property Acts of 1870 and 1882 had reformed coverture in legal theory, yet inside a rural English community where the law lagged the legislation by another generation.

• Building sentence (anthology context + comparison axis named): Browning's dramatic monologue stages a Victorian speaker who takes property-marriage logic to its lethal endpoint, while Mew's dramatic monologue stages an early twentieth-century rural farmer whose ordinary cruelty exposes the slower domestic damage of the same social arrangement; both poets adopt the same form to anatomise possessive love, but their endpoints reveal opposite registers of how the social structure permits or restrains the violence implicit in it.

• Thesis: Across form (cinquain dramatic monologue versus irregular-stanza dramatic monologue), structure (linear narrative culminating in murder versus seasonal decline culminating in fragmentation), and language (triple repetition of "mine, mine" versus single direct rupture "Not near, not near!"), Browning presents possessive love as a lethal logic the form preserves while Mew presents it as an ordinary cruelty the form exposes, producing in the reader retrospective horror at one and sustained discomfort at the other.

• Comparative Topic sentence: While Browning encloses possessive love inside the closed surface of a continuous-cinquain dramatic monologue whose articulacy preserves the lethal logic, Mew breaks the same dramatic-monologue form across irregular stanzas whose deteriorating shape exposes the ordinary cruelty its speaker cannot recognise.

• Comparative Technique: Browning's cinquain dramatic monologue with hymn-like ABABB rhyme contrasts with Mew's irregular-stanza dramatic monologue laced with West-Country dialect register; Browning quotes "That moment she was mine, mine, fair, / Perfectly pure and good" inside the polished pentameter cinquain, while Mew's "Three Summers since I chose a maid, / Too young maybe — but more's to do" opens the irregular monologue in vernacular speech — both poets adopting dramatic monologue, but their formal containers register opposite stances toward the speaker's self-understanding.

• Comparative Evidence: Where Browning's repeated "mine, mine" sits within an unbroken cinquain whose ABABB closes neatly on every stanza, Mew's "chose a maid, / Too young maybe" sits inside a stanza whose rhyme-scheme will shift from couplets to alternating rhyme to triplet across the poem — articulate completion as opposed to deteriorating form.

• Comparative Close Analysis: Browning's Latinate "Perfectly" (from perfectus, finished) sounds the speaker's syntactic confidence at the moment of murder, while Mew's parenthetical "Too young maybe" registers a flicker of self-doubt the farmer immediately overrides with practical harvest logic; one form preserves articulacy across violence, the other exposes bewilderment despite it.

• Comparative Effects: Thus Browning's cinquain form forces the reader into mounting horror at a speaker whose composure never cracks, while Mew's irregular form produces sustained discomfort at a speaker whose bewildered tone registers ordinariness rather than monstrosity — claustrophobic complicity through closed form, ambient implication through fracturing form.

• Comparative Author's Purpose: Browning may have chosen the cinquain dramatic monologue to expose the speaker's articulacy as the moral indictment itself, while Mew can be read as adopting the irregular dramatic monologue to expose the social arrangement rather than the individual — both poets weaponising the form, the choice between continuity and deterioration carrying the difference.

• Comparative Context: Browning wrote in 1836 under the full regime of coverture codified in Blackstone's Commentaries, the dramatic monologue inherited from Romantic-period experimentation; Mew wrote in 1916 fifty years after the Married Women's Property Acts had reformed coverture in legal theory but inside a rural community where property-marriage practice endured — the same form mediated by opposite stages of the same social arrangement.

• Comparative Topic sentence: Where Browning structures the poem as a linear narrative driving toward the murder as climactic event, Mew structures hers as seasonal decline drifting toward closing fragmentation that refuses to narrate the violence the form implies.

• Comparative Technique: Browning's linear-chronological structure with strangulation-as-climax pairs against Mew's seasonal-decline structure with closing-fragmentation; Browning builds toward "And strangled her. No pain felt she" through unbroken forward motion, while Mew's "The short days shorten and the oaks are brown... Her eyes, her hair, her hair!" drifts from autumn into fragmented body-part listing — both poets organising the poem around a pivotal violent moment, but the structures register opposite relations to that moment.

• Comparative Evidence: Where Browning's "In one long yellow string I wound / Three times her little throat around" arrives as a precise climactic event the form delivers, Mew's closing "Oh! my God! the down, / The soft young down of her... her eyes, her hair, her hair!" arrives as collapse into obsessive repetition — climax as opposed to disintegration.

• Comparative Close Analysis: Browning's measurement "Three times" registers the strangulation as deliberate calculation the structure has been preparing across the poem's linear movement, while Mew's repetition "her hair, her hair" registers the breakdown of rational sequence as the farmer's composure collapses into syllabic obsession — calculated linearity dissolving into structural fragmentation.

• Comparative Effects: Therefore Browning's reader experiences retrospective horror once the closing "And yet God has not said a word!" clarifies that the speaker has spent the night beside a corpse, while Mew's reader experiences suspended dread at the threshold of further violence the form refuses to write — recognition as opposed to suspension, the two structures producing distinct registers of how the violence is encountered.

• Comparative Author's Purpose: Browning may have structured the poem as Victorian sensation-narrative inverted into psychological case-study, while Mew can be read as structuring hers around modernist temporal fragmentation that mirrors the farmer's mental deterioration — both poets shaping the violence through structure, the choice between climactic completion and fragmenting suspension encoding the difference.

• Comparative Context: Browning's 1836 structure reflects early-Victorian interest in forensic psychology and sensation narrative, both refining linear case-study form; Mew's 1916 structure draws on modernist experimentation with temporal fragmentation that her queer-and-marginalised perspective made available — the same dramatic monologue mediated by opposite structural traditions across eighty years.

• Comparative Topic sentence: While Browning's language perverts religious vocabulary into the speaker's self-justification, Mew's language applies hunting vocabulary to a human being to expose marriage itself as legalised capture.

• Comparative Technique: Browning deploys religious-register vocabulary fused with triple repetition, while Mew deploys hunting-register vocabulary fused with collective pronoun; Browning quotes "Porphyria worshipped me; surprise / Made my heart swell" alongside "That moment she was mine, mine, fair, / Perfectly pure and good", while Mew quotes "We chased her, flying like a hare / Before our lanterns... We caught her, fetched her home at last" — religious vocabulary converting murder into preservation versus hunting vocabulary converting marriage into capture.

• Comparative Evidence: Where Browning's "worshipped" and "Perfectly pure and good" anoint the dead beloved as sacred object, Mew's "chased," "caught," "fetched" convert the bride into prey before the collective "we" implicates the community as accomplice — religious anointing of a corpse as opposed to communal hunting of a wife.

• Comparative Close Analysis: The triple "mine, mine, fair" registers possession at its grammatical limit through repetition alone, while Mew's hunting verbs "chased, caught, fetched" register acquisition through animal-economy diction; both lexicons dehumanise the woman, the first through religious aestheticisation and the second through pursuit logic.

• Comparative Effects: Indeed Browning's reader experiences chilled revulsion at the conversion of murder into devotion through liturgical syntax, while Mew's reader experiences visceral discomfort at the conversion of marriage into livestock acquisition through everyday vernacular — sanctification as opposed to commodification.

• Comparative Author's Purpose: Browning may have inverted religious diction to expose how a possessive imagination sanctifies its own violence, while Mew can be read as deploying hunting diction to expose how rural property-marriage normalises its own brutality through the language a community shares — both poets weaponising lexicon, the registers carrying the difference.

• Comparative Context: Browning's 1836 religious vocabulary leaned on the rhetorical authority of Victorian Christianity at its public peak, the speaker's pseudo-piety contemporary readers could not miss; Mew's 1916 hunting vocabulary drew on Edwardian rural-English speech in which animal-husbandry economics still framed marriage in the field — the same property-marriage critique mediated by opposite linguistic registers.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Browning and Mew both stage possessive love through the dramatic monologue's signature exposure-by-articulation, but the endpoints along which their speakers stand produce opposite tonal registers: Browning's speaker completes the violence his monologue prepares while Mew's farmer remains suspended on the threshold the poem refuses to narrate.

• Controlling concept: Possessive love in the cluster operates not as a stable category but as a phenomenon whose endpoint depends on the social arrangement permitting it — and the question of whether the form preserves or fragments at the moment of violence is the cluster's deepest dramatic-monologue insight.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Browning may have written to expose the lethal endpoint of property-marriage logic in its early-Victorian intensity, while Mew can be read as writing the slower domestic damage of the same arrangement in its rural twentieth-century endurance — both poets refusing the cultural permission their speakers assume.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two reveals that the cluster's deepest move on possession is the recognition that the social arrangement, not the individual, produces the violence — and that the dramatic-monologue form is the cluster's clearest demonstration that listening to the powerful speak is the surest way to expose the power they assume.

Q2 — The Farmer's Bride (predicted anchor) [HIGH confidence — never set as anchor in 9-year window]

Frame

Mew's 1916 dramatic monologue and Browning's 1836 dramatic monologue both anatomise power and control inside marriage, but their speakers register opposite registers of self-understanding: Mew's farmer is bewildered by his own cruelty while Browning's speaker is articulate about his violence. The plan traces three layers of the differential — form (irregular dramatic monologue versus cinquain dramatic monologue), structure (seasonal decline culminating in fragmentation versus linear narrative culminating in murder), and language (hunting vocabulary applied to a human versus religious vocabulary perverted).

Pedagogical depth to thread. Coverture and property-marriage logic at two historical moments. Mew writes in 1916 — fifty years after Married Women's Property Acts reformed coverture in legal theory but inside a rural community where the older arrangement persisted. Browning writes in 1836 — fully thirty-four years before even the limited 1870 reform. Both poems anatomise the same legal-and-social structure across different stages of its transformation.

Technique gloss: As Q1. Integrated-comparison shape unchanged (BP1 = Form / BP2 = Structure / BP3 = Language) — the anchor switches to Mew so the comparative topic sentences open "While Mew..., Browning..." rather than "While Browning..., Mew...".

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): The Married Women's Property Acts of 1870 (limited reform) and 1882 (general reform) ended coverture — the legal doctrine under which a married woman's legal identity merged into her husband's — yet when Charlotte Mew published The Farmer's Bride in 1916, rural English marriages were still often economic arrangements in which the woman's consent was secondary, the law on the statute books outpaced by the practice in the field by another generation.

How should I greet thee? — / With silence and tears" organises the poem around the "silence and tears" returning at the close unchanged from the opening — philosophical leap as opposed to social stasis.

• Comparative Evidence: Where Hardy's structural opening on the afternoon and structural closing on the catalogue "Your face, and the God-curst sun, and a tree, / And a pond edged with grayish leaves" delivers the philosophical claim that one scene has permanently encoded itself, Byron's structural opening on "silence and tears" and structural closing on the identical phrase delivers the recognition that nothing has changed — philosophical accretion as opposed to stuck repetition.

• Comparative Close Analysis: Hardy's structural movement from "that winter day" to "Since then" and beyond registers the cyclical return reaching across years, while Byron's structural repetition of "silence and tears" registers the cyclical return refusing to advance from its origin; one structure travels and returns enriched, the other returns identical.

• Comparative Effects: Therefore Hardy's reader experiences cosmic disillusionment at the structural reach across decades that has reconfigured the apparatus of perception, while Byron's reader experiences constrained anguish at the structural stasis that confirms the wound has not healed across the imagined re-encounter — cosmic disillusionment as opposed to social stasis.

• Comparative Author's Purpose: Hardy may have structured the poem around the cyclical return to register the anti-Romantic refusal of consolation, while Byron can be read as structuring his around the cyclical return to register the doubled wound of Regency aristocratic affair where social exposure renews the parting's grief — both poets engineering the structure around what the closing return should reveal.

• Comparative Context: Hardy's 1867 structure draws on the cyclical-return tradition of late-Victorian naturalism (Hardy's own "The Voice", "The Going") that uses repetition to register permanent change; Byron's 1815-16 structure draws on the cyclical-return tradition of Regency-era popular ballad (Burns's "My Heart's in the Highlands", Coleridge's "Frost at Midnight") that uses repetition to register stuck emotion — the same theme mediated by opposite structural traditions.

• Comparative Topic sentence: While Hardy's language deploys anti-Romantic pathetic fallacy that refuses consolation, Byron's language deploys polyptoton inside Regency aristocratic register that performs and resents the codes of masculine stoicism.

• Comparative Technique: Hardy deploys anti-Romantic pathetic fallacy inside alliterative coiling, while Byron deploys polyptoton inside biblical-sense vocabulary; Hardy quotes "And the sun was white, as though chidden of God, / And a few leaves lay on the starving sod" alongside "And wrings with wrong, have shaped to me", while Byron quotes "They know not I knew thee, / Who knew thee too well" alongside "Long, long shall I rue thee, / Too deeply to tell" — pathetic-fallacy-refused-consolation as opposed to polyptoton-with-biblical-knowing.

• Comparative Evidence: Where Hardy's "chidden of God" registers cosmic disillusionment (the universe itself scolded and silenced), Byron's polyptoton "know-knew-knew" registers the doubled wound of privileged intimacy and isolated grief — cosmic register as opposed to social register.

• Comparative Close Analysis: Hardy's phrase "wrings with wrong" creates the sound of an agonised tormented fissure and puns on the wedding ring the relationship never produced, while Byron's polyptoton "know-knew-knew" combines the biblical sense (carnal knowledge) with social knowledge to capture privileged intimacy in a society that denies recognition; one lexicon registers cosmic disillusionment through alliterative coiling, the other registers social humiliation through grammatical variation.

• Comparative Effects: Indeed Hardy's reader experiences cosmic disillusionment at language that withholds consolation where Wordsworth or Coleridge would have nature console, while Byron's reader experiences constrained anguish at language that performs and resents the aristocratic-stoicism code the form requires — cosmic refusal as opposed to social-coded performance.

• Comparative Author's Purpose: Hardy may have deployed anti-Romantic pathetic fallacy to register the late-Victorian crisis of religious faith where God's silence was indistinguishable from God's absence, while Byron can be read as deploying polyptoton-and-aristocratic register to register the gap between felt grief and performed grief Regency conditions demanded — both poets weaponising lexicon, the registers carrying opposite conditions of voice.

• Comparative Context: Hardy's 1867 anti-Romantic language draws on late-Victorian naturalism that rejected the Romantic idea that nature sympathised with human emotion; Byron's 1815-16 polyptoton-language draws on Regency aristocratic codes that demanded masculine stoicism filtered through ballad form — the same theme mediated by opposite linguistic-cultural conditions of how grief was permitted to speak.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Hardy and Byron both stage lost love through cyclical structure that argues grief permanently encodes itself, but their narrators occupy opposite positions on the philosophical-reach-versus-social-entrapment axis — Hardy leaping from single afternoon to claim about all subsequent reality while Byron remains trapped within the aristocratic register's constrained anguish.

• Controlling concept: Lost love in the cluster operates not as a single emotion but as a feeling whose articulation depends on the conditions of voice the form imposes — and the question of whether the speaker reaches beyond the immediate moment or remains trapped within it is the cluster's deepest differential between late-Victorian naturalism and Regency aristocratic register.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Hardy may have written the anti-Romantic refusal of consolation in which nature offers no softening, while Byron can be read as writing the doubled wound of Regency aristocratic affair — both poets arguing that some grief is permanent, but they register opposite cultural conditions of how grief is permitted to speak.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two reveals that the cluster's deepest move on lost love is the recognition that grief's permanence is shaped by the social-cultural register that permits or constrains its articulation — and that the closed loop's two registers (philosophical, aristocratic) are both forms the cluster gives to permanent emotional damage.

Two critical voices to deploy

Sophicly deploys two named critical voices across the L&R corpus. Use them sparingly — once per essay maximum — and only when they sharpen the close analysis.

• William Dowling (Rutgers) on the dramatic monologue tradition — attribute as "Rutgers critic" or "Rutgers lecturer". The reading: Browning's speakers (in Porphyria's Lover and My Last Duchess from the parallel anthology) are "untouchable because they have wealth, power and ancestry"; their articulate composure encodes willingness to kill rather than yield. Use in Q1, Q2, Q3 (where the dramatic-monologue tradition is the formal centre).

• Helen Vendler on form-as-meaning. The reading: form is the first argument a poem makes; when the form fits the content, the poem reinforces its argument; when the form fights the content (Barrett Browning's rule-broken sonnet about love overflowing its container; Armitage's curtal sonnet about curtailment; Mew's irregular stanzas about a relationship in deterioration), the contradiction IS the argument. Use across any Q where formal differential is the analytical event — particularly Q1, Q2, Q4, Q5, Q6, Q9.

(Attribute readings to these named voices; do not name Mr Bruff in essays. Bruff is a teaching guide for revision, not an academic source for citation.)

Final reminder bullet

• Name WHICH love. Love and Relationships is multivalent; every comparison thesis must specify which love the two poems anatomise (romantic-erotic, possessive, parental, filial, familial-by-blood, familial-by-marriage / cross-cultural, mourned, long-distance).

• Frame property-marriage logic historically, not feministically. Use coverture / property-marriage law / dynastic marriage / honour culture rather than "patriarchy" or "patriarchal society"; the critique of the social arrangement stays intact, the vocabulary stays historicist.

• One anchor quote per BP — single line, embodying the topic-sentence concept.

• Discourse markers when switching poets — Thus / Whereas / By contrast / However / Indeed / In conclusion.

• Hedged author's-purpose claims — may have / can be read as / a defensible reading is.

• No "shows" as analytical verb — illustrates / portrays / registers / enacts / dramatises / exposes / reveals.

• No arrows — through / from / leading to / which produces.

• No magic / spellbook framing — describe technique as deployed / used / dropped / inverted, not as cast or conjured.

• Hook must tie thesis — fact / quote / question / metaphor that encapsulates THIS essay's specific argument.

• Sentence cap ~35-45 words / ~3 lines. Split rather than truncate.

• Topic sentences are pure concept — sentence 1 names no techniques, not even forms; technique-naming moves to sentence 2.
